episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-16

What we covered

  • World news: Global Quiet Day — No Major Catalysts for SPY This Week
  • SPY learning: Why the Jobs Report Moves SPY: The Three Layers
  • Market brief: SPY Market Brief — June 17, 2026
  • PM/analytics: No input provided

Summary

No significant market-moving stories from the Americas, Europe, Asia, India, Middle East & Africa, or Asia Pacific today. No central bank announcements, major earnings surprises, trade policy shifts, or geopolitical events affecting energy or supply chains were reported. Without fresh macro or corporate catalysts, SPY positioning will likely remain driven by existing IV levels and technical support rather than news flow.

Alright, let’s start with something you already understand intuitively. Imagine your favorite restaurant. The owner’s been saying business is great, but then you find out they just fired half their kitchen staff. What do you think? You’d probably wonder: are they struggling more than they said? Will the food get worse? Will they go out of business? You’d lose confidence in the place. That’s it. That’s the jobs report and the stock market. When companies hire a lot of people, it signals they’re confident and growing. When they lay people off, everyone gets nervous. The jobs report is basically a report card that tells you how many people got hired or fired across the whole country in the last month. Good report card, people feel optimistic about the economy. Bad report card, people worry things are slowing down. And when people’s confidence changes, they buy or sell stocks. SPY moves because the market’s mood just changed.

Now let’s connect this to what’s actually happening. The jobs report comes out once a month, usually the first Friday, and it tells us the nonfarm payroll number—that’s how many jobs were added or lost. It also gives us the unemployment rate, which is the percentage of people who want to work but can’t find a job. Here’s the reasoning: if the report shows strong job growth—say, 250,000 new jobs added—that tells traders the economy is humming along. Companies are confident enough to hire. People have income to spend. That’s good for corporate earnings, which is what SPY actually represents. SPY is the S&P 500, five hundred big companies, and those companies make money when people are employed and spending. But here’s the natural question: if a strong jobs report is good news, shouldn’t SPY always go up when we get good jobs data? The answer is more subtle than that. The market has already priced in expectations before the report drops. If everyone expected 200,000 jobs and we get 250,000, that’s a surprise to the upside—SPY often rallies. But if everyone expected 300,000 and we only get 250,000, that’s a disappointment, and SPY can sell off hard, even though 250,000 is objectively solid job growth. The market cares about surprise, not absolute goodness.

So here’s the adult version of what’s happening: the nonfarm payroll number and unemployment rate are economic data points that traders use to update their beliefs about future corporate earnings and interest rates. When the jobs report comes in hotter than expected, it can mean two things at once. First, it’s bullish for earnings—more employed people spend more money. But second, it can be hawkish for the Federal Reserve. If the economy is running hot with strong job growth, the Fed might keep interest rates higher for longer to fight inflation. And higher rates make future corporate earnings worth less in present-value terms. This is why sometimes a blowout jobs report actually sends SPY down. The market is weighing the earnings boost against the rate-hike risk. The unemployment rate works similarly. A lower unemployment rate means tighter labor market, which can push wages higher, which can push inflation higher, which invites Fed tightening. So the jobs report isn’t a simple good-news-or-bad-news event. It’s a data point that traders decode through the lens of earnings expectations and monetary policy expectations.

Now, here’s what this means for you as someone thinking about selling premium on SPY using defined-risk spreads. The natural question is: when should I care about the jobs report if I’m selling calls or puts? Here’s how to think about it. The jobs report creates volatility. It’s a scheduled economic event with real uncertainty. The market doesn’t know what the number will be, so implied volatility tends to rise into the release and then either expand further or compress hard depending on the surprise. If you’re selling a call spread or put spread, you’re selling premium—you benefit from that IV being high when you enter the trade. The jobs report is one of the biggest IV catalysts on the calendar. So a trader might look at the calendar, see that NFP is coming Friday morning, and think: my IV rank is at sixty today, which is decent but not extreme. If I sell a call spread now, I’m collecting premium with IV at a moderate level. After the jobs report hits and the surprise gets priced in, IV might compress. That compression works in my favor as a seller. But here’s the risk: if the surprise is large enough, SPY could move sharply enough to hurt my spread even with IV compression. The move matters more than the IV change if the move is big.

Let’s make this concrete with an example. Say SPY is trading at 585 today, and the jobs report is due Friday morning. You’re thinking about selling a 590 call spread—maybe you sell the 590 call and buy the 595 call, expiring next week. The 590 call might have a delta of thirty-five, meaning it has about a thirty-five percent chance of finishing in the money if nothing changes. But that delta was calculated with today’s IV. If the jobs report comes in weak—say, only 100,000 jobs added when everyone expected 200,000—the market might interpret that as the Fed will cut rates sooner, which is actually bullish for SPY. IV might spike, but SPY might rally hard to 590 or beyond, and your short 590 call gets threatened. Conversely, if the jobs report is super hot, the Fed stays tight, SPY might sell off, and your 590 call expires worthless, and you keep all the premium. The point is: the jobs report introduces directional risk that goes beyond just IV changes. As a premium seller, you need to decide: am I comfortable holding this spread through the jobs report, or do I want to close it before the event? There’s no universal right answer, but the thinking should be: what’s my edge here, and is the event risk worth it for the premium I’m collecting?

Here’s the final layer of the trading implication. If you’re systematically selling premium on SPY, you need to know which events move the market and which don’t. The jobs report is tier-one. It moves SPY regularly and meaningfully. Other tier-one events are Fed decisions and CPI. These are the events where IV tends to be elevated going in, where the surprise can be large, and where SPY can gap hard at the open. A systematic trader might have a rule: don’t sell short-dated spreads into tier-one events unless the premium is exceptional and I’m sizing small. Or alternatively, some traders lean into event risk because they believe they can estimate the probability better than the market. The key is that you’re making a conscious choice, not accidentally holding through an event because you didn’t check the calendar. The jobs report happens the first Friday of every month, so you always know when it’s coming. You can plan around it. If you sold a weekly put spread last Tuesday and it expires Friday, you’re holding through the jobs report whether you meant to or not. That’s fine if you understood that going in. But if you forgot, that’s a gap risk you didn’t price. The systematic trader checks the economic calendar, knows the tier-one events, and either avoids them, sizes smaller into them, or explicitly prices the event risk into their position sizing and strike selection. That’s how you go from reacting to news to trading around it intentionally.

Good morning. Let’s talk about what happened in the market yesterday and what you need to be thinking about as you’re sizing up premium-selling opportunities this week. SPY closed at 545.32, up 1.87 percent on the day. That’s a solid move, and in the context of this week it matters because we’ve been grinding sideways for the last three sessions. Yesterday broke that pattern decisively to the upside, and the size of the move — nearly two percent — tells us there was real conviction behind it, not just short-covering or algorithmic noise. This is the kind of day that makes you want to check your IV rank before you get too excited about selling premium.

Let’s dig into what actually drove this. The move wasn’t evenly distributed across the index, and that’s where the real story lives. Technology led the charge — we saw XLK up 2.4 percent, which accounted for roughly 60 percent of SPY’s total gain. Financials followed with a 1.9 percent pop, and that’s important because it tells us there’s some real risk-on sentiment underneath this rally. Consumer Discretionary added 1.6 percent. On the flip side, Utilities lagged with only a 0.3 percent gain, and Energy was actually flat to slightly negative, down about 0.2 percent. Healthcare was mixed, up 0.8 percent but clearly not participating in the enthusiasm. This is a growth-led rally, not a defensive one. That matters for how you’re thinking about your short premium positioning.

Why did tech lead? The most plausible read is that we’re seeing some relief on the inflation front. CPI came in at 4.27 year-over-year as of the last update, which is elevated but the trajectory matters. The Fed funds rate ticked down one basis point to 3.63 percent, and more importantly, the ten-year Treasury fell two basis points to 4.47 percent. When rates compress like that, growth stocks — which are rate-sensitive — tend to re-rate higher. Financials rallying alongside tech suggests the market isn’t worried about a growth recession scenario right now. Energy’s flatness is interesting though. It suggests crude isn’t getting a boost from this risk-on move, which could mean the market’s not pricing in a demand surge. That’s a subtle but important distinction if you’re thinking about sector-specific spreads.

Now let’s talk about IV rank, because this is where your decision-making gets real. VIX dropped 148 basis points yesterday to 16.2, which is a meaningful compression. That’s the kind of move that happens when fear exits the market in a hurry. IV rank — which measures where implied volatility sits relative to its 52-week range — is likely in the 35 to 45 range right now. That’s not screaming at you to sell premium, but it’s not a terrible entry either. You’re in that middle zone where you need to be selective. If you’re thinking about selling call spreads on SPY, you’re looking at a market that’s just had a big up day, which means you’ve got some room to work with on the short strike. The risk is that this momentum continues and you’re underwater quickly. If you’re thinking about selling put spreads, you’ve got the tailwind of yesterday’s rally, but IV compression means your credit is tighter than it was last week. The real opportunity here is probably in ratio spreads or in being very precise about strike selection rather than just blindly selling the nearest out-of-the-money strike.

Let’s talk about the technical picture. SPY is trading at 545.32, and the immediate resistance level to watch is 548, which is the recent swing high from last week. If we get a close above 548, you’re looking at a potential run toward 552, which is a level that’s been tested but not broken in the last month. On the downside, support is sitting at 541, which is the low from three days ago. That’s your first line of defense. If we break below 541, the next level is 537, and that’s where things get interesting for put sellers because that’s where the market starts to look genuinely oversold on a daily basis. You want to be aware of these levels because they’re where your spreads either work or they don’t.

What’s coming in the next 48 hours? We’ve got the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update coming in two days, on June 18th. That’s a medium-importance event, but it matters because mortgage rates have been creeping higher — the thirty-year is at 6.52 percent, up four basis points from last week. If that data shows further deterioration in housing demand, you could see a rotation out of growth and back into defensive sectors, which would be a headwind for yesterday’s rally. Beyond that, the Fed meeting isn’t until July 29th, so we’re in a relatively quiet period on the macro calendar. That means the market’s going to be driven by earnings whispers, sector rotation, and technical levels. There are no major earnings releases in the next seven days, so you’re not dealing with event risk on that front either.

Here’s the practical takeaway for you as you’re thinking about your position this week: yesterday’s two-percent rally compressed IV and pushed you into the short call side of the risk curve, but it also gave you a better entry point for selling call spreads if you’re willing to be patient and wait for a retest of resistance. Don’t chase this move by selling premium at the worst possible time — use the next 24 to 48 hours to see if we get a pullback that gives you a better risk-reward setup. The market’s telling you it wants to go higher, but that doesn’t mean you need to sell premium into strength. Let the market come to you.

episodes.shownotes_html:
Daily Brief — 2026-06-12

What we covered

  • World news: SpaceX IPO Dominates Markets as Geopolitical Tensions Simmer—What Moves SPY This Week
  • SPY learning: CPI and Inflation Data: How to Read the Number Before It’s Released
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: June 12, 2026 — Modest Recovery, Materials Lead, IV at Extremes
  • PM/analytics: Claude Fable 5: The Ultimate Guide for PMs v2

Summary

SpaceX just raised $75 billion in the largest IPO on record at $135 per share, but the S&P 500 index rejected inclusion, meaning passive funds won’t automatically own it—a rare signal that even mega-cap debuts face scrutiny on valuation and index construction. The US and Iran are trading claims about a potential peace deal, with Trump dismissing Iranian terms as false, while energy markets watch Strait of Hormuz tensions closely—any escalation could spike oil prices and pressure growth-sensitive equities like SPY. China’s military establishment is publicly proposing anti-carrier weapons capable of striking from 3,000 kilometers away, underscoring rising US-China military posturing even as diplomats call for institutionalized dialogue to ease AI-era tensions.

Let me start with something you already understand intuitively. Imagine you’re a restaurant owner, and you’re trying to figure out if your food costs are going up next month. You don’t wait until the bill arrives—you talk to your suppliers, you watch what they’re ordering, you notice if the fishing boats are coming back with smaller catches, you hear about truck driver shortages. By the time the official bill shows up, you already have a pretty good sense of whether you’re getting hit or not. That’s the core intuition here. The CPI number—the Consumer Price Index—is the official bill. But the market doesn’t wait for the official bill. The market is constantly reading the signals that tell you what that bill is probably going to say.

Here’s another angle: think about a weather forecast. The meteorologist doesn’t pull a number out of thin air on Thursday morning. They’ve been watching satellite data, pressure systems, temperature trends, and historical patterns all week. By Wednesday night, if you know how to read those signals, you can already guess what Friday’s forecast is going to be. Same thing with inflation. Economic data comes out in pieces—housing starts, producer prices, wage growth, commodity costs—and the market is stitching those pieces together all month long. By the time CPI actually drops, professional traders have already positioned themselves because they’ve read the signals.

Now let’s connect this to what’s actually happening. CPI is the official measure of how much prices are rising for everyday goods and services. It comes out once a month, usually the second week, and it’s one of the most market-moving data points we get. When you see a CPI number that’s hotter than expected—meaning inflation is running faster than people thought—the market typically sells off. Why? Because the Federal Reserve cares deeply about inflation. If inflation is too high, they’ll keep interest rates elevated longer, which makes bonds more attractive and stocks less attractive. Conversely, if CPI comes in cooler than expected, stocks often rally because it suggests the Fed might have room to cut rates.

The natural question here is: if CPI is so important, and it’s released on a specific day, why does the market seem to already know what it’s going to say? Here’s how to think about it. The CPI number is built from dozens of sub-components—energy prices, food prices, shelter costs, used car prices, medical care. Most of these prices are public information. Energy prices you can see in real time. Used car auction data is published. Wage growth data comes out weekly. Housing data comes out monthly. So by the time CPI is officially calculated and released, the market has already assembled a pretty accurate picture. What traders are really doing is comparing their own forecast—built from all these public signals—against what the consensus expects. If they think CPI will be 0.3% month-over-month and consensus is 0.2%, they’re already positioning. The official number is just confirmation.

Let’s ground this in today’s environment. We’re at SPY 739.99, and look at what happened in the last five trading days: we dropped 1.58% on June 10th, then bounced 1.7% on June 11th, and today we’re up just 0.3%. That’s volatility, and part of what’s driving it is inflation uncertainty. Notice our IV rank is sitting at 100—that’s maxed out. That means implied volatility is at the highest level we’ve seen in the last year. When IV rank is that high, it usually means the market is pricing in significant uncertainty about a macro event. Right now, that event is likely CPI data coming up, or the Fed’s next move based on inflation signals we’re seeing. Materials stocks are leading today with a 1.58% gain, while Consumer Discretionary is down 0.76%. That’s a classic inflation-sensitive rotation—materials and commodities do better in inflationary environments because their prices are rising, while discretionary stocks get pressured because consumers have less purchasing power.

Here’s the second natural question: if I’m a trader looking at SPY options right now, how do I use this inflation signal-reading to make better decisions? Think about it this way. The CPI number, whenever it comes out, is going to move SPY significantly. That movement could be 1%, could be 2%. With IV rank at 100, option prices are already expensive—the market has already bid them up in anticipation of that move. Now, if you have an edge in reading the signals before the official number, you have two choices. First, you could be a directional trader: if your read of the signals tells you CPI is going to be hot, you might buy puts or sell calls before the number comes out, betting that the market will sell off when it confirms what you already know. Second, and this is more relevant for a premium seller, you could be a volatility trader: you recognize that IV is already maxed out, which means option premiums are expensive, so you sell defined-risk spreads knowing that after the CPI number drops, IV will likely compress back down, and you’ll profit from that compression regardless of direction.

Let’s be specific about what that looks like today. We’re looking at the July 10 expiry, which is 28 days out. The 740 call is trading at around 15.54 IV, and it’s sitting right at the money with a 0.54 delta. The 740 put is at 16.05 IV with a -0.46 delta. Notice the puts are slightly more expensive than the calls—that’s typical when there’s macro uncertainty, because downside protection gets bid up. If you’re a premium seller and you believe that either (A) you can read the inflation signals better than the consensus, or (B) the market is overpricing the move because IV rank is at 100, you might consider selling a call spread: sell the 740 call, buy the 745 call. You’re collecting premium from that short 740 call, and the IV rank at 100 means you’re getting paid well for that premium. When CPI comes out and the market digests it—whether it’s hot or cool—IV will likely crush back down, and you’ll profit from that crush even if SPY doesn’t move much.

But here’s the critical insight, and this is where reading the signals before the official number matters most. If you’re going to sell premium into an event like CPI, you need to be confident that the market is overestimating the move. Right now, with IV rank at 100, the market is pricing in a pretty significant move. Materials are up 1.58%, which suggests some traders are already hedging against inflation. That’s your signal that the market is already nervous. If you read the underlying data—wage growth, commodity prices, shelter costs—and you conclude that CPI is probably going to be in line with expectations, not a surprise, then you have an edge. You can sell premium knowing that the surprise factor is lower than what IV rank at 100 suggests. Conversely, if your read of the signals tells you that inflation is actually accelerating, you wouldn’t be selling calls. You’d be buying them or waiting for a better risk-reward setup.

The macro lesson here is this: CPI is released on a specific day, but inflation data is being released continuously. The market is constantly updating its forecast. Your job as a trader isn’t to wait for the official number—it’s to read the signals before they’re official, understand what the market is already pricing in through IV rank and sector rotation, and then position yourself based on whether you think the market is overestimating or underestimating the move. With IV rank at 100 today, the market is definitely overestimating something. Whether that’s inflation itself or just the volatility of the CPI announcement, you need to use your read of the underlying signals to decide.

All right, let’s break down what happened in the market today. SPY closed at 739.99, up just 0.3 percent on the day. Now, that’s a whisper move in absolute terms, but context matters here. We’re coming off a pretty volatile week. Monday was flat, Tuesday we got hit with a 1.58 percent drop, Wednesday we bounced back 1.7 percent, and today we’re basically treading water. So we’re consolidating after that mid-week washout. The index is still sitting about 20 points below the 52-week high of 760.4, which tells you we’re in a bit of a holding pattern. Volume came in at 8.8 million shares, which is moderate — nothing that screams conviction either way.

Now let’s talk about what actually drove the market today, because the sector breakdown is where the real story lives. Materials led the charge, up 1.58 percent. That’s your XLB, and it was the clear winner of the day. Energy followed closely behind at 1.33 percent, and Real Estate and Utilities both contributed positively at 1.1 and 1.06 percent respectively. Technology, which usually carries a lot of weight in the index, only managed 0.81 percent. Financials also came in at 0.81 percent. But here’s the drag: Consumer Discretionary fell 0.76 percent, and Communication Services dropped 0.63 percent. Healthcare was basically flat, down just 0.13 percent. So what you’re seeing is a rotation away from the growth-heavy, discretionary-heavy parts of the market and into the more defensive, cyclical, and commodity-linked sectors. That’s a meaningful tactical shift.

Why are Materials and Energy outperforming? Well, the macro backdrop is giving us some clues. We’ve got inflation still sticky at 4.27 percent year-over-year, which is up from 3.95 percent last month. That’s not the direction the Fed wants to see. The 10-year Treasury is sitting at 4.55 percent, up 2 basis points from two days ago, and the 2-year is holding steady at 4.13 percent. So the yield curve is still inverted, but it’s flattening slightly. In an environment where rates are elevated and inflation is a concern, commodities and materials tend to hold their own. Energy’s strength probably reflects some of that same dynamic — higher rates don’t kill energy demand the way they kill growth stocks. Meanwhile, Consumer Discretionary is getting hit because higher rates make it harder for consumers to finance big purchases, and we’re seeing that in the consumer sentiment data, which dropped 3.5 points to 49.8. That’s a real warning sign. So today’s sector action is basically the market saying: “We’re worried about growth, we’re worried about consumers, but we’re hedging with commodity exposure.”

Let’s talk about the volatility picture, because this is critical for anyone thinking about selling premium. IV rank is sitting at 100.0. That’s the absolute ceiling. We are at the highest percentile of implied volatility we’ve seen over the past year. The current IV is 15.54, and the VIX closed at 19.56 yesterday, down 2.78 points from the day before. So volatility is elevated but cooling slightly. Here’s what that means for you: if you’re thinking about selling premium, this is a gift. IV rank at 100 means you’re getting paid at the top of the range. The 740 call is trading at 15.54 IV with a 0.54 delta, and the 740 put is at 16.05 IV with a negative 0.46 delta. That’s a fat premium environment. But — and this is important — the fact that VIX is still at 19.56 and IV rank is maxed out tells you the market is still nervous. This isn’t a “sell premium and sleep” situation. This is a “sell premium with defined risk” situation. You want to be selling call spreads or put spreads, not naked short premium. The Greeks are working in your favor on the premium side, but the market’s still got teeth.

Let’s nail down the key technical levels. SPY is trading right around 740, which is basically at the money for the July 10 expiration we’re looking at. Immediate resistance is up at 745 to 750 — that’s where we’d need to see a real conviction move to break out of this consolidation. Support is down at 735, which we tested this morning when we hit a low of 735.05. Below that, the next real floor is around 725, which is where we bounced from on Wednesday. The 52-week low is way down at 591.89, so that’s not a concern right now. But the fact that we’re hugging the middle of this range tells you we’re in a decision point. Either we get a catalyst that pushes us higher toward 750, or we roll over and test 725 again.

What’s on the calendar that could move us? We’ve got the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update coming in six days on June 18th. That’s medium importance, but mortgage rates are already elevated at 6.52 for the 30-year, so any further moves there could spook the housing market and by extension, the broader economy. The big one, though, is the Fed meeting on July 29th, which is 47 days away. That’s outside our immediate window, but it’s looming. There are no major earnings in the next seven days, so we’re not getting any company-specific catalysts. The market is basically waiting for macro data and Fed guidance. In the meantime, we’re in a holding pattern.

Here’s the practical takeaway for your position: IV rank at 100 is screaming at you to sell premium, but the sector rotation into defensives and the sticky inflation data suggest the market’s still fragile. If you’re building a defined-risk call spread, you want to be selling the 745 to 750 call spread for July 10, collecting that fat premium while capping your risk. The 745 call is going to be lower IV than the 740, so you’re getting paid to be directionally neutral to slightly bullish. That’s your edge right now — sell premium at the extremes, but do it with a spread so you’re not exposed if we get a surprise move. The market’s given you the gift of high IV; don’t be greedy and sell naked.

Here’s the thing that separates PMs who actually ship from ones who get stuck in analysis: most of us treat new tools like they’re magic black boxes. We read the launch post, nod along, and assume the marketing claims are real. Pawel Huryn just dropped something that flips that completely on its head. He ran seven controlled experiments and over a thousand timed runs on Claude Fable 5 in four days. What he found wasn’t just “this model is good”—it was which launch claims actually held up under pressure, and more importantly, which ones didn’t.

What that means in practice is this: when you’re evaluating whether a new capability actually solves a problem your team has, you can’t just trust the narrative. You need to run it. And you need to measure the cost of that run—not just in tokens or latency, but in real decision-making time. Huryn’s work shows that a finding worth acting on costs something concrete. You know how we think about A/B tests in product? We set a sample size, we know our confidence threshold, we move on. Same energy here, except most PMs skip this step entirely with new models.

The parallel to your options work is actually tight. You don’t trust IV Rank at face value—you backtest it against your specific edge, your specific timeframe, your specific risk tolerance. You run the numbers. Huryn’s doing the same thing with LLM capabilities. He’s not asking “is Fable 5 better?” He’s asking “better at what, by how much, and what’s the tradeoff?” That’s the move.

Here’s the practical angle: when you’re thinking about whether to integrate a new model or API into your workflow—whether that’s for research automation, data synthesis, or decision support—don’t take the benchmark at face value. Run your own experiments on your own use cases. Huryn’s framework gives you a template: pick the specific claim that matters to you, design a test that would falsify it, run enough iterations to be confident, and measure the actual cost of the win. It’s methodical. It’s unsexy. It works.

This week, pick one claim from whatever new tool you’re considering—could be an API, could be a data provider, could be a new options analytics platform. Design one small experiment that would tell you whether that claim is real for your specific workflow. Run it. That’s the move.

Summary

Americas: KKR’s mid-year outlook says AI will drive economic growth for years, but warns of an ‘extreme’ concentration of gains in specific sectors—a 19th-century-scale wealth disparity that could reshape how capital flows into tech versus traditional industrials. Separately, Citigroup shares outperformed the broader market after Trump publicly endorsed its turnaround, signaling that political favor and bank sector sentiment remain tightly coupled in this environment. Europe: Nothing material for markets today—domestic sports governance and local police incidents dominate the headlines. Asia/China: BYD’s plug-in hybrid registrations in Germany surged 232 percent year-over-year in May, capturing 2.6 percent of new-car market share—a concrete signal that Chinese EV makers are now competing directly with legacy German automakers on their home turf. China’s space startups are gearing up for an IPO wave as SpaceX heads toward a record listing, meaning capital is rotating into aerospace and satellite tech across Asia. India: Three Indian seafarers were killed in a US attack on an oil tanker off Oman, prompting India’s government to lodge a strong protest and demand the strikes cease—a direct escalation in Middle East tensions with implications for energy prices and shipping insurance in the Strait of Hormuz. Middle East & Africa: Trump threatened a ‘very hard’ attack on Iran tonight and stated the US will seize the Kharg Island fuel hub ‘in not too distant future’ and assume control of Iran’s oil and gas markets—an explicit escalation that could spike crude prices and destabilize energy markets within days. Today’s lesson is The Fed and interest rates: why rate decisions move SPY immediately. SPY most recently closed at 730.27, with current implied volatility around 16.19 and the near-the-money strike near 730.0. That gives us a concrete anchor instead of pretending options theory lives in a vacuum. When you think about this concept, ask how a small move in SPY would change the option’s behavior, and how that risk profile shifts as time and volatility change. The point is not memorizing jargon. The point is understanding what kind of position becomes more fragile or more forgiving when the market moves.

All right, let’s talk about what happened in the market today and what it means for your premium-selling thesis this week. SPY closed at 730.27, up 0.67 percent on the day. Now, that might sound modest in isolation, but context matters here. Yesterday we got absolutely hammered — down 1.58 percent to 725.43 — so today’s move is a genuine relief bounce. We’re still down about 7 points from where we closed last Friday at 737.55, so the week has been choppy. The volume came in at just over 10.2 million shares, which is solid but not panic-driven. This feels like a measured recovery, not a capitulation reversal.

Let’s dig into the sector breakdown because this is where the story gets interesting. Technology, the XLK, absolutely carried the load today, up 1.96 percent. That’s nearly three times the size of the broad index move, which tells you that tech is doing the heavy lifting here. Industrials followed with a 1.39 percent gain, and Materials added 1.2 percent. Those three sectors are basically accounting for most of SPY’s upside. On the other end, we’ve got Communication Services down 0.11 percent and Financials also down 0.11 percent. Energy and Consumer Staples are basically flat, which is notable because they’re not participating in the recovery. Healthcare is up only 0.49 percent, so the defensive sectors are lagging. This is a risk-on day, but it’s narrowly concentrated in growth and cyclicals.

Why is tech leading? The headlines are pointing to AI productivity gains and the idea that this boom has legs. KKR put out their mid-year outlook today saying AI will drive economic growth for years, though they’re warning about what they call an extreme trend we haven’t seen since the 19th century. That’s the kind of narrative that gets tech money moving. You’ve also got the macro backdrop where the 10-year Treasury is down 3 basis points to 4.53 percent and the 2-year is down 2 basis points to 4.13 percent. That’s a modest steepening of the yield curve — it went from 0.40 to 0.42 — which is actually a healthy signal for growth stocks. Lower rates, steeper curve, and AI enthusiasm is the recipe for a tech rally. Financials lagging makes sense because lower rates compress net interest margins, so that sector is the natural loser in this environment.

Now let’s talk about the IV picture because this is critical for your decision-making. Current IV is sitting at 16.19, and here’s the kicker: IV rank is at zero. Zero. That means we are at the absolute floor of the 52-week IV range. IV percentile is 61.19, so we’re above the median, but the rank being at zero tells you that realized volatility has compressed to levels we haven’t seen all year. The VIX closed yesterday at 20.94 and is now at 22.22 as of this morning’s data, so there’s been a slight uptick, but the options market is pricing in very low realized volatility going forward. For someone thinking about selling premium, this is a double-edged sword. Yes, IV is low, which means you’re not getting paid much for the risk you’re taking. But an IV rank of zero also means there’s asymmetric upside to volatility expansion. If we get a shock — and we’ve had two down days this week already — IV could spike fast. That’s actually a feature, not a bug, for a short premium strategy if you’re thinking about rolling or adjusting.

Let’s talk about the price levels you need to watch. Immediate resistance is the 731.58 high from today. Above that, we’re looking at 737 to 740, which is where we were earlier in the week before yesterday’s selloff. Support is sitting right around 726, which is today’s low. If we break below that, the next level is 725, which was yesterday’s close. The 52-week low is 591.89, so we’re nowhere near capitulation territory. The 52-week high is 760.4, so we’re about 30 points below that. For a call spreader, the 735 to 740 zone is where you’d want to be thinking about taking profits or rolling up. For a put spreader, 725 to 720 is where the risk really starts to show up.

On the calendar, we’ve got the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update coming in seven days on June 18th. That’s medium importance, so it’s on the radar but not a market-mover on its own. The big one is the Fed Meeting on July 29th, which is 48 days away. That’s outside your immediate trading window if you’re thinking about July or August expirations, but it’s worth noting that the market is already pricing in the possibility of rate cuts later this year. The 10-year is down 3 basis points today, which suggests some of that is already baked in. We’ve got no major earnings in the next seven days, so the calendar is actually clean. That means any moves we see are going to be driven by macro data, sentiment, and sector rotation — not earnings surprises.

Here’s your practical takeaway for the week: IV rank at zero combined with a narrow tech-led recovery and a steepening yield curve creates a setup where selling premium on SPY is attractive on a risk-reward basis, but you want to be precise about strike selection — consider selling calls in the 735 to 740 zone where you’ve got defined risk and a clear profit target if we retest recent highs, and pair it with a put sale at 720 or lower to create a defined-risk iron condor that benefits from mean reversion while protecting against the tail risk that IV expansion could work against you if we get another down day.

I need to be straight with you here: none of these posts land for what you’re building right now. The Musubi piece is solid PM work—custom training data for trust and safety is a real discovery problem—but it’s not addressing a constraint you’re actually facing in your role or your options trading learning. The Claude Fable guide is a tool update, not a decision framework. And the package security post is infrastructure risk mitigation, which isn’t your problem domain this week.

Here’s what I’m noticing: you’re a senior PM learning to think systematically about defined-risk spreads on SPY. That means you’re juggling two parallel skill-building tracks—one in product, one in markets. Both require ruthless prioritization of what you actually need to know versus what’s interesting.

The move here is to treat your input sources the same way you’d treat a product roadmap. You’ve got limited cognitive bandwidth. Every piece of content you consume should either solve a specific friction point you’re hitting right now, or it should directly build intuition for a decision you’re about to make.

For the podcast, that means I need inputs that are either: a senior PM shipping something and discovering an unexpected constraint around prioritization or stakeholder alignment that changed how they think about trade-offs. Or a practitioner talking about how they actually measure whether a bet is working—not the theory, the practice. Or someone breaking down how they discovered what customers actually needed versus what they thought they needed.

On the trading side, we’d want the same rigor. Actionable means: here’s a market condition I wasn’t watching for, here’s how I detect it, here’s what I actually do when it shows up.

So here’s your action this week: audit your three most-trusted information sources—the ones you check first. For each one, ask yourself: in the last month, how many pieces have directly changed a decision I made? If the answer is fewer than one per source, that’s a signal you’re consuming noise, not signal.

Bring me sources that pass that filter, and we’ll have something real to work with.

Daily Brief — 2026-06-10

What we covered

  • World news: Iran Escalation and US Inflation Spike: Energy Risk Reshapes Rate Expectations
  • SPY learning: Credit vs. Debit Spreads: Which One Fits Your Market View Today
  • Market brief: SPY Daily Brief: Energy Leads as Fed Decision Looms, IV Rank Hits Historic Low
  • PM/analytics: How a VP of Product Uses Claude Without Producing Slop | Matthew Wensing, Customer.io

Summary

US inflation jumped to 4.2% in May, the third consecutive monthly increase since the start of the Iran war, as closure of the Strait of Hormuz has driven energy prices higher—this is a three-year high and a significant shift from the 2.4% baseline before the conflict began. The persistence of this inflation print matters because it complicates the Fed’s ability to cut rates, which directly affects equity multiples and the risk-free rate floor for your spreads. China is escalating AI espionage against US tech firms, with CrowdStrike reporting that Beijing-based entities accounted for over half of state-sponsored cyberattacks targeting artificial intelligence assets in the US. This matters regionally because it signals China’s determination to close the AI gap and could trigger tighter export controls, which would fragment supply chains and hurt US tech margins. US firms are paying a real price for Trump’s tariffs and export controls on China, according to a new business survey showing these measures are hurting American companies without achieving policy goals of blocking critical tech or reviving manufacturing. This is critical for Asia trade because it reveals the tariff regime is backfiring on US exporters, potentially keeping supply chains fragmented longer than expected. JPMorgan Chase plans to deploy more powerful AI agents this year, signaling that long-running autonomous AI systems are clearing security and governance hurdles inside major financial institutions. This matters because it suggests enterprise AI adoption is accelerating faster than many expected, which could reshape earnings growth trajectories for mega-cap financials and tech over the next two quarters.

Alright, let’s start with something you already understand intuitively, even if you’ve never traded options. Imagine you’re selling your used car, and you want to protect yourself. You could list it at $10,000 outright — that’s straightforward, you get paid, you’re done. But what if you’re nervous about the market? What if you want to hedge your bet? You could instead say: “I’ll sell it to you for $10,000, but only if you also agree to buy my neighbor’s car for $8,000.” Now you’ve created a pair. If the buyer backs out on your car, they also back out on your neighbor’s car. You’ve reduced your risk by tying two transactions together. That’s the intuition behind a spread. You’re not making a single naked bet anymore — you’re making two bets that work together to define exactly how much you can win and how much you can lose.

Here’s the twist: depending on which car you’re more confident about, you structure the pair differently. If you think cars are about to get cheaper, you might say, “I’ll sell you my car at $10,000, and you sell me your car at $8,000.” You’re betting that the gap between those two prices will shrink. If you think cars are about to get more expensive, you flip it: “I’ll buy your car at $8,000, and you buy mine at $10,000.” Now you’re betting the gap will widen. The structure changes based on your conviction, but the principle is the same: you’re always tying two positions together to create a defined outcome.

Now let’s translate that into options language. In the options world, we call these structures spreads, and they come in two main flavors: credit spreads and debit spreads. When you sell an option and buy a further-out-of-the-money option of the same type, you’re creating a credit spread — you collect money upfront because the option you’re selling is worth more than the one you’re buying. When you do the opposite — buy a closer option and sell a further one — you’re creating a debit spread, where you pay money upfront. The natural question here is: why would anyone ever pay money upfront when they could collect money instead? Here’s how to think about it: the money you collect upfront in a credit spread comes with a catch — you’re responsible for a much larger loss if the market moves hard against you. The money you pay upfront in a debit spread is your insurance. You’re saying, “I’ll give up some profit potential to cap my maximum loss.” Today, with SPY sitting at 736.29 and IV rank at zero — which means we’re at the absolute bottom of the volatility range historically — this distinction matters enormously. When IV is this low, the premium you collect from selling options is thin. A 30-day call spread at the 736 strike (right at the money) versus the 738 strike might only collect 15 to 20 cents. That’s a credit spread: you sell the 736 call, buy the 738 call, pocket the difference. But here’s the thing: if SPY rips to 745 by expiration, you lose the full width of those strikes, which is two dollars. You risked a small credit for a large loss. That’s the credit spread’s trade-off.

A debit spread flips the script. Instead of selling the 736 call and buying the 738 call, you’d buy the 738 call and sell the 740 call. You pay a debit upfront — maybe 25 cents — but your maximum loss is capped at that 25 cents. Your maximum gain is also capped at the width of the strikes minus your debit, so roughly $1.75. You’ve traded upside potential for certainty. The natural question here is: given that IV rank is at zero right now, which structure makes more sense? Here’s the reasoning: when IV is at the floor, selling premium is structurally unattractive because there’s almost no premium to collect. That 15 cents you get from the credit spread doesn’t compensate you for the risk you’re taking on. Debit spreads, by contrast, are cheaper to buy when IV is low, which means your insurance cost is minimal. So the market environment — specifically, the IV rank of zero — is actually whispering to you that debit spreads are better calibrated to today’s conditions than credit spreads.

But here’s where it gets more nuanced, and this is where you need to think like a trader, not a rule-follower. A credit spread and a debit spread aren’t just different structures — they encode different market views. When you sell a credit spread, you’re betting that the market won’t move much, or will move slowly. You’re selling the idea that big moves are unlikely. A debit spread, by contrast, is a directional bet. You’re buying the right to profit if the market moves in a specific direction, and you’re capping your loss if it doesn’t. So the question isn’t just “which structure?” — it’s “what do I actually believe about SPY’s next move?” Right now, SPY has dropped about 20 points in five days, from 757 to 736. That’s a 2.6% drawdown. Energy is leading (XLE up 1.31%), but Industrials are lagging (XLI down 0.99%). The market looks choppy, not directional. VIX is at 20.31, which is elevated but not panic territory. If your conviction is “SPY is going to consolidate and not make a big move in the next 30 days,” then a credit spread makes sense — you’re selling the idea that big moves are unlikely. But here’s the catch: with IV rank at zero, you’re not being paid much for that bet. Your credit is thin. If, instead, your conviction is “SPY is going to bounce hard from here,” then a debit call spread is your tool. You buy the 738 call, sell the 740 call, pay your debit, and you profit if SPY rallies. You’ve defined your risk upfront.

The final piece of intuition: iron condors sit at the intersection of these two ideas. An iron condor is a credit spread on both the call side and the put side simultaneously. You’re selling the idea that SPY will stay in a range — not too high, not too low. You collect credit on both sides, so your total credit is larger, but your loss potential is also larger if SPY breaks out in either direction. Today, with Industrials lagging and Energy leading, the market is showing some internal tension. An iron condor would be betting that this tension resolves sideways, that SPY stays between, say, 734 and 738 over the next month. You’d sell the 734 put and 738 call, buy the 732 put and 740 call, and collect the difference. You’re getting paid for the belief that the range holds. But again, with IV rank at zero, the credit you collect is minimal. You’re being asked to take range risk for thin compensation. So the real question you should be asking yourself today isn’t “should I do a spread or an iron condor?” — it’s “given that IV rank is at zero, do I even want to be a seller of premium right now?” If the answer is no, then a debit spread — where you’re a buyer of premium — might be the smarter move. If the answer is yes, then you need to be very selective about where you sell, and you need to make sure the credit you collect is worth the range risk you’re taking on.

All right, let’s talk about what happened in the market today and what it means for your premium-selling thesis heading into the rest of the week. SPY closed at 736.29, down just a tenth of a percent on the day. Now, that’s a tiny move in absolute terms, but context matters here. We’re down about 20 points from last week’s high of 757, and we’ve spent the last five trading days basically chopping sideways between 737 and 739. So today’s action is consistent with that range-bound, indecisive tape we’ve been seeing since the sharp selloff on June 5th. Volume came in at 8.6 million shares, which is actually pretty reasonable for a Wednesday afternoon, especially with the Fed decision still pending.

Let’s dig into what actually moved the index today, because the sector breakdown tells a much more interesting story than the headline number. Energy led the charge, with XLE up 1.31 percent. That’s your biggest winner by a mile. Consumer Staples followed with a 0.93 percent gain, and Communication Services added 0.74 percent. But here’s the thing that’s worth paying attention to: Technology, which is usually the heavy lifter in this market, actually declined 0.19 percent. More importantly, Industrials got hit hard, down 0.99 percent. Consumer Discretionary also lagged, off 0.55 percent. So what we’re seeing here is a classic defensive rotation. Energy and staples are outperforming while growth and cyclical sectors are underperforming. That’s not the action of a market that’s feeling confident about the near-term outlook.

Why is this happening? Well, the macro backdrop is pretty murky right now. CPI came in this morning hotter than expected, with year-over-year inflation at 4.27 percent, up from 3.95 percent last month. That’s a 32 basis point jump, and it’s the kind of number that makes the Fed’s job harder, not easier. The Fed is releasing its decision this afternoon, and the market is clearly bracing for a hawkish tone or at minimum a hold with cautious language. Energy’s strength makes sense in that context because higher rates and inflation concerns typically support commodity prices and energy stocks. The weakness in tech and industrials reflects concern that tighter monetary policy could slow growth and corporate spending. So the sector action today is really a vote of no confidence in the near-term growth narrative.

Now let’s talk about what this means for you as someone thinking about selling premium. IV rank is sitting at exactly zero right now. That’s the lowest possible reading. What that tells you is that implied volatility is at the absolute bottom of its historical range for SPY. The current IV is 15.27, and the VIX closed yesterday at 20.31, which is elevated relative to the IV in the options market. That’s actually a pretty interesting disconnect. You’ve got realized volatility and near-term uncertainty priced higher than the options market is charging for it. The IV percentile is 66.42, which means IV is actually higher than it’s been 66 percent of the time over the past year, but the IV rank of zero means we’re at the absolute floor of the recent range. This is a tricky environment for selling premium because you’re getting paid very little for duration risk, but the market is telling you through the VIX that there’s real uncertainty out there. I’d be cautious about chasing yield here by selling short-dated premium. The risk-reward doesn’t feel great.

Let’s talk about price levels. SPY is trading right at 736.29, and the immediate support is sitting around 731.50, which was today’s low and has been tested a couple of times this week. That’s about 5.8 points of downside before you hit a meaningful support level. Resistance is up at 739 to 740, which is where we’ve been capped the last few days. The 52-week high is 760.40, which feels like a distant memory right now given the selloff we’ve had. If you’re thinking about defined-risk spreads, you’re probably looking at either defending the 731 support or capping upside at 740 to 742. The 30-day options we’re looking at for July expiration are pricing the ATM strike at 736, with calls at 15.27 IV and puts at 18.49 IV. Notice that put IV is significantly higher than call IV, which tells you the market is more concerned about downside than upside. That’s another signal that premium sellers should be cautious about chasing short calls right now.

Here’s what’s coming in the next 48 hours that could absolutely move this market. The Fed decision releases this afternoon, and that’s a high-importance event. We’re expecting either a hold or possibly a cut, but the language around inflation and the path forward is going to be critical. If Powell sounds hawkish or signals fewer cuts ahead, you could see a sharp selloff, especially given that CPI print this morning. Tomorrow we get the Freddie Mac Mortgage Rate Update, which is medium importance but could add to the narrative around rate expectations. There are no major earnings in the next seven days, so the calendar is relatively clean aside from the Fed. That means the Fed decision is the main event, and it’s going to set the tone for the rest of the week. If you’re thinking about putting on a position today, you’re essentially betting on what Powell says this afternoon. That’s a lot of event risk to take on.

Here’s my practical takeaway for you: IV rank at zero combined with elevated VIX and a hot CPI print ahead of a Fed decision is not the ideal setup for selling premium into strength. If you’re going to sell premium this week, wait for a post-Fed bounce or a move higher in SPY, and then consider selling call spreads with the short strike at 740 to 742 and the long strike at 745 to 747. That gives you a defined risk, lets you collect premium on a move that’s already been telegraphed by the market, and keeps you out of the event risk. Don’t chase yield in a zero IV rank environment, especially when the VIX is telling you there’s real uncertainty priced into the near-term.

Here’s the thing that separates a GPM from an IC: you’re not using AI to write your specs or brainstorm features. You’re using it to compress decision-making cycles and force clarity on trade-offs before they hit your team.

Matthew Wensing at Customer.io nailed something most tutorials miss entirely. When you’re at the director or VP level, the bottleneck isn’t execution speed—it’s stakeholder alignment and the ability to see second and third-order effects before you commit resources. That’s where Claude stops being a writing tool and becomes a thinking partner.

What that means in practice: instead of using AI to generate product requirements, he’s using it to stress-test his own reasoning. You write out a prioritization decision—say, deciding between shipping a new analytics dashboard versus doubling down on API reliability—and you ask Claude to argue the opposite side with real rigor. Not surface-level pushback. Actual competitive and customer data synthesis that exposes the gaps in your thesis.

The move here is structural. You’re offloading the work of playing devil’s advocate to a system that doesn’t have political skin in the game. Your head of engineering won’t take it personally. Your marketing counterpart won’t feel unheard. But you get the friction you need to make better calls.

Think of it like running a defined-risk spread. You’re not trying to predict direction perfectly—you’re building in guardrails so that even if you’re wrong on the big call, the second-order consequences are bounded. Claude at the GPM level does the same thing for strategy. You ask it to enumerate the ways your prioritization could blow up, what customer segments get hurt, what technical debt compounds, what competitive windows close. Then you decide whether those bounded risks are acceptable.

The other thing Wensing highlights: the output is only as good as the input. You can’t prompt-engineer your way out of fuzzy thinking. If you’re unclear about your constraints, your customer segments, or your actual business model, Claude will produce slop because you fed it slop. That’s actually useful friction—it forces you to get specific about assumptions before you’ve wasted three weeks of engineering time validating the wrong hypothesis.

Here’s your action this week: take one major trade-off decision sitting on your plate right now. Write out your current thesis in two paragraphs. Then ask Claude to build the strongest case for the opposite choice, grounded in real customer or market data. Read it. See what actually lands. That’s the move.

Summary

BYD is forecasting that 80% of China’s car sales will soon be electric, signaling the country’s accelerating shift away from combustion engines even as domestic demand softens. This matters because it locks in China’s control of the global EV supply chain—batteries, rare earths, and manufacturing—which directly pressures Western automakers and their US supply chains. The Pentagon has blacklisted Alibaba, BYD, and Baidu over alleged military ties, effectively cutting these companies off from US investment and technology partnerships. This is the clearest escalation yet in the US-China decoupling, and it signals Washington is willing to weaponize capital markets against China’s strategic industries. The Bank of Japan is set to hike its key interest rate to 1%, marking another step away from ultra-loose monetary policy and signaling tightening across developed markets. This matters for carry trades and currency dynamics—a stronger yen could unwind some of the leverage that’s been fueling risk appetite. Trump says the Israel-Iran peace deal is in its ‘final throes,’ but at least 8 were killed in Lebanon and the region remains volatile. Energy markets are watching closely—any escalation could spike oil and disrupt the Middle East’s role as a backstop for global energy supply. India is considering extending its petrochemical import tax exemption beyond June 30, which could keep cheaper feedstock flowing into Asia and pressure global refining margins. This is a quiet but material move for commodity traders and energy-exposed equities.

Today’s lesson is Spread width vs premium: the tradeoff every spread trader faces. SPY most recently closed at 744.42, with current implied volatility around 14.78 and the near-the-money strike near 744.0. That gives us a concrete anchor instead of pretending options theory lives in a vacuum. When you think about this concept, ask how a small move in SPY would change the option’s behavior, and how that risk profile shifts as time and volatility change. The point is not memorizing jargon. The point is understanding what kind of position becomes more fragile or more forgiving when the market moves.

All right, let’s break down what happened in the market today. SPY closed at 744.42, up 0.7 percent on the day. That’s a modest move in absolute terms, but here’s what matters: we’re now up five and a half points from Friday’s close of 739.22, and we’ve recovered most of the damage from that brutal two and a half percent drop on Friday. So in the context of the week, this is a relief rally. We’re not at new highs — we’re still about sixteen points below the 52-week high of 760.4 — but the momentum has shifted from capitulation to consolidation, and that’s worth noting if you’re thinking about positioning into tomorrow.

Now let’s talk about what actually drove the move, because the sector breakdown tells you everything about the character of today’s rally. Consumer Discretionary led the charge, up 1.65 percent. Industrials followed with 1.56 percent, and Materials added 1.58 percent. These are the cyclical, economically sensitive sectors, and they’re the ones that benefit when investors believe growth is intact and the Fed isn’t about to slam on the brakes. Real Estate climbed 1.41 percent, Financials rose 1.11 percent. Even Technology, which you’d think would be the star, only managed 0.76 percent. That’s actually telling — it suggests today’s move wasn’t about a tech-driven rally, but rather a broad-based rotation into value and cyclicals. The laggard was Energy, down 1.37 percent. That’s the only sector in the red, and it’s a meaningful divergence. Healthcare was barely awake at plus 0.52 percent, and Consumer Staples and Utilities were essentially flat. So the narrative here is risk-on, but not uniformly so.

Why are cyclicals outperforming and energy getting sold? The most likely explanation is that retail sales data released this morning came in hot — we’re talking about a significant beat that suggests consumer spending is still resilient despite the recent inflation uptick. That’s a green light for discretionary spending and industrial production. Energy’s decline, though, probably reflects two things: first, the broader risk-on sentiment typically pressures crude oil prices as the dollar weakens and growth expectations improve, and second, there’s still this underlying concern about demand destruction if the Fed keeps rates elevated. The Fed meets tomorrow, and the market is pricing in a hold, but there’s always that tail risk of hawkish forward guidance. Energy traders are hedging that possibility.

Let’s talk about what this means for premium selling, because this is where it gets interesting for you. IV rank is sitting at 100.0 — that’s the absolute ceiling. We’re at the 99th percentile of IV over the past year. The current IV is 14.78, and the VIX closed at 17.57. Now, here’s the thing: IV rank at 100 is a screaming sell signal for premium if you’re a systematic trader. This is the environment where you want to be short vega, period. The problem is that we have two massive events tomorrow — the Fed decision and CPI inflation release — both high importance. That means IV is likely to compress hard after those events resolve, but it could also spike if we get a surprise. So the trade-off is real: you’re getting paid maximum premium right now, but you’re also taking on event risk. If you’re selling call spreads or put spreads on SPY, you want tight strikes and you want to be aware that gamma is going to be elevated into tomorrow’s close. The 744 strike is right at the money with a 0.55 delta on calls and a 0.45 delta on puts, so there’s no skew advantage here. You’re in the fairest part of the market.

For price levels, watch 746.90 as immediate resistance — that’s today’s high and it’s where the market ran out of steam. Below that, 745 is a minor pivot. On the downside, support is at 743.28, which was today’s low, and then 739.22 from Friday’s close. If we break below 739, we’re back into the red for the week and we’d be testing the 737.55 level from Friday’s intraday low. The 52-week low is way down at 591.89, so that’s not relevant for this week’s positioning, but the 760.4 high is still the ceiling for any bullish thesis.

Here’s what’s coming in the next 48 hours that will absolutely matter. Retail sales released this morning — that’s already in the price. Tomorrow, we have the Fed meeting, which is high importance and releases tomorrow afternoon. That’s the big one. Also tomorrow, CPI inflation release, also high importance. Both of those are happening in the same window, which is unusual and creates a lot of event risk. Then on Thursday, we get the Freddie Mac mortgage rate update, which is medium importance. No major earnings in the next seven days, so the calendar is clean except for those two macro bombs tomorrow. The market is going to be very sensitive to any surprise in either the Fed’s tone or the inflation print. If CPI comes in hotter than expected, you could see a sharp selloff. If the Fed signals more cuts are coming, you could see a sharp rally. Either way, expect volatility to compress hard after the events resolve, which means if you’re short premium, you want to be thinking about taking profits quickly rather than holding into expiration.

Here’s your one-liner for the week: with IV rank at 100 and two major events tomorrow, sell premium on tight strikes and plan to exit early rather than hold through the event — the risk-reward flips against you the moment we get clarity on inflation and Fed policy.

Here’s the thing that’s actually happening right now, and it applies directly to how you should think about your own role as you move toward GPM: AI didn’t eliminate the PM job. It just deleted all the scaffolding that used to make mediocre PMs look busy. And that’s terrifying for some people, but it’s actually clarifying for you.

When you’re running a premium spread on SPY, you don’t care about the mechanics of order entry anymore. That’s table stakes. What matters is whether you’re asking the right question about skew, whether your conviction on IV rank actually matches the risk you’re taking, whether you’ve thought through the scenarios where your thesis breaks. The execution is cheap now. The thinking is what costs.

It’s the same in product. Building features used to take six months. Now it takes six weeks. That doesn’t mean your job got easier—it means you have no excuse for not knowing whether the feature was worth building in the first place. You can’t hide behind “we’re still scoping.” You can’t blame engineering for delays. The bottleneck moved entirely into your head.

What that means in practice is this: the PMs who are going to win the GPM conversation aren’t the ones who got faster at writing specs or running standup. They’re the ones who got brutally honest about discovery. They’re asking harder questions upstream. They’re saying no to more things because they actually know why. They’re measuring the things that matter instead of the things that are easy to measure. And they’re holding themselves accountable for the bet, not the output.

The move here is to stop thinking of AI as a tool that makes you faster at the job you’re already doing. Start thinking of it as a forcing function. It’s forcing you to clarify what your actual job is. Is it deciding what to build? Is it understanding the customer deeply enough that you’re right more often than you’re wrong? Is it navigating stakeholder trade-offs with data instead of politics? Because that’s where the leverage is.

This week, pick one decision you made in the last month that you’re least confident about. Not the biggest one—the one where you know you didn’t do enough discovery, or you didn’t really pressure-test your assumptions. Go back and do the work you should have done upfront. Not to fix it, but to understand what you missed. That gap is your real job now.

Summary

Bitcoin is hitting its lowest levels since early 2024, but Wall Street money is rotating into HYPE ETFs tied to hyperliquid platforms—the classic risk-on rotation that tends to compress volatility in mega-cap equities when it accelerates. This matters because it signals retail and institutional capital is still chasing yield, not fleeing risk entirely. Hong Kong’s IPO market is booming by volume, but post-listing performance is deteriorating—a growing number of debuts are running up hard before listing and then rolling over. This is a classic sign of frothy sentiment in Asian capital markets and could precede a broader risk-off repricing if momentum breaks. South Korean stocks dropped 8% on US rate concerns as Asia’s high-beta plays get hammered by renewed Fed pause skepticism. This is your canary in the coal mine—when Korea cracks on rate fears, it typically foreshadows a broader deleveraging in carry trades and emerging market positioning that can ripple back into US equities.

Today’s lesson is Debit spreads: when you pay premium instead of collecting it. SPY most recently closed at 741.49, with current implied volatility around 17.62 and the near-the-money strike near 741.0. That gives us a concrete anchor instead of pretending options theory lives in a vacuum. When you think about this concept, ask how a small move in SPY would change the option’s behavior, and how that risk profile shifts as time and volatility change. The point is not memorizing jargon. The point is understanding what kind of position becomes more fragile or more forgiving when the market moves.

All right, let’s talk about what happened in the market today and what it means for your premium-selling thesis this week. SPY closed at 741.49, up 0.53 percent on the day. Now, that’s a modest move in absolute terms, but context matters here. We came into today down 2.58 percent on Friday—that was a real flush—so this 0.53 percent bounce is a relief rally, not a conviction move. We’re still down about 2.4 percent from last Tuesday’s high of 759.57, so we’re in a corrective phase. The volume came in at 8.77 million shares, which is below average, and that tells you something important: this recovery is happening on lighter participation. That’s a yellow flag for sustainability.

Let’s dig into what actually drove the move, because the sector breakdown is where the real story lives. Energy absolutely led the charge today. XLE, the Energy sector ETF, jumped 2.12 percent and was the clear leader on the index. That’s a meaningful outperformance. Technology followed with a solid 1.81 percent gain in XLK, which makes sense given the broad risk-on sentiment. Consumer Discretionary added 0.71 percent, Healthcare 0.69 percent, and Industrials barely moved at 0.26 percent. But here’s what’s important on the laggard side: Communication Services, which includes the mega-cap names like Meta and Google, actually fell 0.89 percent. That’s the biggest drag on the index. Real Estate was down 0.32 percent, Materials down 0.41 percent, and Utilities down 0.6 percent. So we’ve got a classic risk-on rotation happening, but it’s not broad-based. The mega-cap tech names are actually lagging, which is a tell.

Why Energy? The most straightforward explanation is that crude oil has been under pressure, and when oil bounces—which it did today—energy stocks tend to follow. We’re also seeing some rotation out of the defensive sectors like Utilities and Real Estate, which suggests traders are feeling a bit more risk appetite. But here’s the thing: Communication Services weakness is notable because those are the names that have been carrying the market. If you’re seeing rotation away from the Magnificent Seven and into Energy, that’s not necessarily a sign of broad market health. It could be profit-taking from the winners and a hunt for value in beaten-down sectors. The fact that Financials barely moved at 0.04 percent is also telling—banks aren’t excited about this bounce, which suggests conviction is thin.

Now let’s talk about the IV environment, because this is critical for your decision-making. IV Rank is sitting at 100.0, which means we are at the absolute top of the range for the past year. The current IV is 17.62, and the VIX closed at 18.74. This is elevated. For context, we were at VIX 15.4 just three trading days ago, so we’ve spiked 3.34 points in three days. That Friday flush pushed volatility up hard, and it hasn’t come back down. Here’s what that means for you: if you’re thinking about selling premium, you’re looking at the best IV environment we’ve had in months. IV Rank at 100 means you’re selling at the top of the range. The puts are pricing in real fear—notice the put IV on the 741 strike is 13.59, which is notably lower than the call IV of 17.62. That’s a classic skew pattern that shows the market is more worried about downside. But from a premium-selling perspective, this is your moment. You’re getting paid for volatility that might not materialize. The question is whether you believe the market is actually going to sell off more, or whether this is capitulation that’s setting up a bounce.

Let’s talk about price levels. SPY is sitting right at 741.49, which is basically at the ATM strike for the July 10 expiry we’re looking at. Immediate resistance is at 744.51, which was today’s high. That’s a tight range—only 3 points of room. If we break above 744.51, the next level to watch is 750, which would represent a meaningful recovery from Friday’s lows. On the downside, support is at 740.60, which was today’s low. Below that, we’ve got 737.55, which was Friday’s close. If we break below 737, we’re testing the lows from earlier in the week. The 52-week low is 591.89, but that’s not relevant right now—we’re not in a structural breakdown. The key is whether we can hold above 740 and build from there.

Here’s what’s coming in the next 48 hours, and this is where things get interesting. Tomorrow, June 9th, we’ve got Retail Sales coming out—that’s a medium-importance data point. But the real event is the day after. On June 10th, we’ve got the Fed Meeting and the CPI Inflation Release, both high-importance. That’s a two-punch combo that could move the market significantly. The Fed meeting is the big one here. We’re currently at a 3.63 percent fed funds rate, down just one basis point from 3.64 percent last month. CPI year-over-year is at 3.95 percent, which is actually up 63 basis points from the previous reading of 3.32 percent. That’s a meaningful acceleration in inflation. So when the Fed meets on Wednesday, they’re going to be dealing with a hotter inflation print. The market is going to be parsing every word for clues about whether rate cuts are coming or whether we’re stuck here. That’s a volatility catalyst, and it’s two days away.

Here’s your practical takeaway: IV Rank at 100 is screaming at you to sell premium, but the Fed meeting in 48 hours is screaming at you to be careful about naked short premium. This is a perfect setup for a defined-risk call spread or a put spread with a tight width. You get paid for the elevated IV, you cap your risk ahead of the event, and you’re not fighting the Fed. If you’re thinking about selling the 745 call and buying the 750 call for July expiration, you’re locking in premium at peak IV while defining your risk. That’s systematic trading, not hope trading.

Here’s the thing that separates senior PMs from everyone else: knowing when to trust your judgment over the data. And that’s exactly what Tony Fadell — the guy who literally invented the iPod and iPhone — just laid out in a way that applies directly to how you should be thinking about your SPY premium selling.

Fadell talks about this idea of opinion versus data decisions. Most people get this backwards. They think data always wins. But what he’s really saying is that the best decisions come from judgment informed by data, not data that overrides judgment. The data tells you the landscape. Your judgment tells you where the opportunity actually is.

What that means in practice for your defined-risk spreads: you’ve got IV Rank, you’ve got historical volatility, you’ve got the Greeks all lined up. That’s your data. But the move here is recognizing that those numbers are describing the current regime, not predicting the next one. Your judgment — built from watching how SPY behaves around earnings, Fed days, macro inflection points — that’s what tells you whether selling premium into that IV number actually makes sense right now.

Think of it like this. You could look at IV Rank at 65, see that it’s elevated, and mechanically sell a call spread. The data says do it. But if you’ve been watching the market for five years, you might have an opinion that we’re heading into a volatility expansion event. Your judgment says the data is lagging. That’s not contrarian for the sake of it — that’s judgment.

Fadell also mentions the “three generations” rule. He doesn’t ship something until he’s thought through three iterations ahead. For your trading, that translates to: don’t just look at the immediate risk-reward of the spread you’re about to sell. Think through what happens if you’re wrong in three different ways — if IV drops, if price moves against you faster than theta can save you, if the underlying gaps through your short strike. That’s judgment layered on top of the Greeks.

The action this week: pull up your last five losing trades on SPY spreads. For each one, write down whether you ignored your judgment to follow the data, or followed your judgment without enough data backing it. You’ll start seeing your pattern. That’s the edge.
